data: Nine lines of Greek text are preserved, almost complete, unevenly centred on the stone.
The letters are neatly and regularly cut, and of standard Hellenistic form, without serifs. Epsilon is notable for having a detached middle bar; beta is not closed.
Antiquarium e sito archeologico di Halaesa

Honorific for [---] Lapiron Sal., son of Apollodoros

The text is regularly cut and well spaced; the terminations to the main strokes are pronounced, but not yet developed into full serifs. There is no space between words. Epsilon has a short middle bar; theta has an unattached middle bar; omicron is full size; alpha consistently has a broken bar; omega is closed and ellipsoid.
late 2nd century or earlier 1st century BCE
honorific for [---] lapiron sal., son of apollodoros

1θεοῖς π[ᾶ]σι
2[ὁ] [δᾶμ]ος τῶν Ἁλαισίνων
3[. c. 6.]Ἀπολλοδώρου Σαλ(- - -)
4Λαπίρωνα
5[εὐνοίας]καὶ εὐεργεσίας ἕνεκ(εν)
6τᾶς εἰς αὑτόν
The first part of all six lines of the Greek text are preserved, but none are complete.

The letters are uniform and well cut, with pronounced serifs. A hollow triangular interpunct is used between all the onomastic elements (but apparently not elsewhere in the text; spaces are however observed between words, with or without interpunct). Theta has a detached middle bar; omicron is full size; epsilon has a detached short middle bar; mu reaches almost to the lower line in the middle, and the outer strokes are vertical.
Photo J. Prag courtesy Soprintendenza BBCCAA di Messina

Very fine to fine-grained, banded, white, calcitic marble, likely Lunense. Optical microscopy: homeoblastic fabric with triple points. Digital microscopy: fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)
Parts of two lines of latin letters are preserved. Substantial vacat below line 2.

The letters are neatly V-cut, of wide proportions, with minimal serifs. P is open; S
                                    has a larger upper half, leaning slightly to the right. Words are separated by a
                                    comma-like three pointed serif.

Fine to medium-grained, homogeneous, white, check marble, likely Thasian-1, Proconnesian. Optical microscopy: homeoblastic to heteroblastic fabric with strained crystals. Digital microscopy: fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)
Parts of three lines of Latin letters are preserved
The letters are neatly V-cut and well-proportioned, with modest serifs. Neither lines 1 nor 3 are completely preserved, but line 1 is taller than line 2, and line 3 is shorter

Dedication by a pair of seviri

Two lines of Latin letters in the field below the moulding (17.5 cm high and
                                        originally 160 cm wide)

The letters are crudely cut (but the stone is too coarse to allow of better): P is
                                    open, but R is closed; Q has a long tail; long I is taller than the other letters
                                    (63mm); E is tall and narrow; words are separated by simple interpuncts.
End 1st century CE, beginning 2nd century CE

Dedication by a sevir to Concordia

Very fine-grained, homogeneous, white, calcitic marble, likely Lunense. Optical microscopy: homeoblastic fabric with triple points. Digital microscopy: homeoblastic fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)
The epigraphic face is preserved for a width of 12.5 cm, with two lines of Latin letters filling the available field.

The letters are tall, neatly cut and closely spaced (height c.30mm), and words are separated by simple triangular interpuncts. The letters are lightly serifed. E is tall and narrow, T has a curved cross-bar, rising to the right.
Late 1st century CE, or 2nd century CE
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Soprintendenza di Messina

Honours for L. Naevius(?) Firminus Manilianus

Very fine-grained, banded and veined, white, calcitic marble, likely Lunense. Optical microscopy: homeoblastic to heteroblastic fabric with triple points. Digital microscopy: homeoblastic to heteroblastic fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)
Remains of four lines of latin text are preserved: only the lower part of the first line survives, and only traces of the tops of letters remain from the beginning of line four.

The letters are V-cut and generously (and sometimes unevenly) spaced, with moderate serifs. R is closed, and both A and R have lightly cut horizontals; all the strokes of M are off-vertical. Words are separated by comma-like interpuncts.
2nd century CE or early 3rd century CE

Dedication of a statue to Ceres, by Iulius Acilius Hermes, sevir

Coarse-grained, veined, white, calcitic marble, likely Naxian. Optical microscopy: heteroblastic fabric with triple points. Digital microscopy: fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)
The Latin text is engraved over two lines (letters c.20 mm high, but of uneven height), to a maximum length of 52 cm.

The letters show some irregularity, with fairly pronounced serifs, and lengthy tails to the letter R. The first two words are separated from the rest by a space; an interpunct is visible at the end of the dedicant’s name, and the final abbreviated phrase is separated from the rest by a large hedera (ivy leaf). The letter I in line 1 is frequently taller than the rest (but so too is the first L in line 1); the final I of line 1 is somewhat irregularly carved.
Statue can be dated stylistically to the late Antonine period (i.e. the 180s or 190s CE), and the text is compatible with such a date.
dedication of a statue to ceres, by iulius acilius hermes, sevir

Fragmentary latin inscription mentioning a proconsul

Fine-grained, veined, grey, calcitic marble, likely Docimian, Pentelic. Optical microscopy: heteroblastic to homeoblastic fabric. Digital microscopy: fabric (identification based on pXRF, digital microscopy, XRD, optical microscopy, stable isotopes analysis)

Letters are 50mm high, tall, thin, serifed. P and R are open; the hastae of M join early and extend upwards; a hedera like interpunct (tail top and bottom to a hollow element) appears once between C and V of line 2.
